title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While walking through the hillside gardens of Stone Court Manor, Hercule Poirot observes the meticulous design of the landscape, noting the rare shrubs and well-maintained paths. As he reflects on the scenery, he recalls previous discussions regarding a murder that occurred years ago in an old quarry near this site. During his walk, Poirot notices a silhouette in the bushes and encounters a tall, slender, and handsome man in his late thirties or early forties.

The man introduces himself as Michael Garfield, the architect of the garden. Garfield explains that the current owners, a retired Colonel and his wife, purchased the unprofitable house at a low price. Garfield reveals that the late Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith, who had financed the garden, left the property to him in her will, though he subsequently sold both the house and the Stone Mine Garden.

As Poirot questions Garfield regarding his motives and his relationship with his former patron, Garfield admits to a level of professional deception. He confesses that while he promised to follow Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith's specific instructions and views, he actually implemented his own artistic ideas by presenting them as her own plans. Garfield justifies this tactic by comparing it to selling a brown egg to a child by framing it as a premium rural product. He asserts that to be a successful artist and businessman, one must skillfully sell their own vision through the guise of a client's blueprints.
